summary: Stancil argues that the modern data stack is consolidating not just in vendor count but in category count: buyers are compressing spending and pushing tools to become broader, more BI-like platforms. As a result, specialized tools either get absorbed, stagnate as niche players, or rebrand into wider enterprise suites, and $10M ARR or “category leader” status no longer signals inevitability.
money_quotes: People stopped looking for specialized tools to solve every problem, and instead started trying to stretch the ones they have across several categories at once.
Software categories aren’t defined by Gartner, or VCs, or marketing taglines; they’re defined by buyers.

My unrelenting belief is that every BI-adjacent product is likely bound for one of three destinations: Be subsumed into some bigger and unabashed BI platform; stall, and become a niche player serving a small market; or get acquired by a major SaaS business that wants to start selling better reporting.

In the data world, I don’t think that’s true anymore. The market is big enough, fragmented enough, and disparate enough that getting to $10 million simply doesn’t—or didn’t, at least—have that much signal anymore.
So what makes a data company inevitable? Honestly, it’s probably the same as it ever was: Repeatable enterprise sales, solving problems in proven categories.
key_insight: Category boundaries in the data stack are collapsing under buyer-driven consolidation, making breadth and enterprise fit more important than niche differentiation or early revenue milestones.
